persons.bioNotes:
Grew up in Upper Darby. Very close with sister. Went to the movies together.

Helped dad with resturant on saturdays. would ride into philly together. didn't know him that well. He worked a lot.

Mother was very strict. Had to do everything her way.

Had a close childhood friend named Billy Cook, Michael McHale - neighborhood people. Used to walk to school together.

In HS, would take the bus to school in West Philly. You'd have tokens/reduced fair. West Catholic High School. Then went to Villanova.

Got a "fellowship" to Nova. Became friends with father Nangioni (sp. ?). Offered John a fellowship for librarian. Went to graduate school for library, although it wasn't acredited. So he went to Drexel instead.

He liked the people and the work of librarians. Sometimes even today (at age 88) he helps out with the library at the retirement home - at the Quadrangle.

People would ask him specific facts, and he would know answers to various topics. People would come to him for help with research papers.

Met Kathleen at a marriage encounter group at the Parish. First friday of every month. They had dancing. Got to meet people with your religion.

Wanted to meet people. early 20s. Had gone to a couple of these before.

Thought Kathleen was beautiful, wonderful personality, got along with everybody.

Eugene Garfield was publishing a reference book called the "Science Citation Index" - said there's an opening for the managing editor. The only downside was that it was far. So he would take the "PTC" (or Septa) to 49th street. Worked there for 2 years. Managed 100+ people. They had to keypunch everything into these ID cards. then another group would do site verification where they would check they key punchers. Edward Write wasn't very good. But if it passed through that, it would go through the publication.

Didn't want to take the bus anymore so quit.

Married Kathleen when he was 30.

Started playing the piano at 8 years old. Could walk to his piano teacher's house. Miss Craig. Asked to learn because he liked piano. Played Oregon at St. Allow's Church. Warsaw Concerto was his favorite piece. 22 pages.

In HS, hard to play piano because of the transportation.

Wedding was very formal, it was a mass. Old latin hyms. Father Rongioni said the mass. Went to Bermuda. Lot of beach days.

Bought house in Upper Darby and moved in with Kathleen right after the honeymoon

Continued the marriage encounter, but fizzled out after children.

Loved parenting. Very good at following routine and structure.

At this point, he worked for Wyatt Labs. Formula division - got a bunch of free instant formula from them.

In charge of the library community. 4 libraries at Wyatt labs - Radnor, Philly, Bluebell, Willowgrove.

IST (institute of scientific information) -> Wyath for 40 years

Travelled a lot. Greece was his favorite vacation.

Kathleen decided that the house was too big so they decided to downsize.

Liked it at the Benson House.



Life lessons: be very visible, be outgoing

He was born John Mitropolous, in the Community of Kakourekia, Greece, son of Dimitrios and Sophia (Gianoukos), and is enrolled in the archives of mail births in Archive No. 2 with a year of birth 1894.  He probably immigrated to this country in the early 20th Century (probably 1906-08).  He arrived in New York and lived with his cousins-in-law in Hartford, Ct.  where he worked at various jobs...mostly in the food business with his cousins.  His dream was to come to this country, work hard and make a new life filled with the opportunities he heard about while a boy in the Peloponises (sp.) Mountains, Greece.  He saved his money and opened a restaurant of his own in Hartford and sent for his brother, Nicholas, who left his wife and family in Greece.  As times were tough, he needed the money.  He did not have the dedication to this country that my Dad had and he went back to Greece with a considerable amount of money.  Being adventurous Dad traveled to 21 states before he married my mother.  After I was born in 1931, my parents opened a restaurant - the St. James - on Euclid Avenue, Cleveland, OH.   I'm not sure about the restaurants he had or their names and dates; but I know he had one in Hartford, Kansas City, MO, and one in Phila. at 5th & Olney Avenue.  This is where he met my mother who came to the restaurant on a date.  He was smitten at once.  In about 1936-37, he opened a restaurant at 30th & Lancaster Avenue, Phila. with his partner Johnny Papas and together they operated the restaurant for about 30 years.  Dad and my mother retired to Fort Lauderdale, FL.  When the Ellis Island Foundation opened their Wall of Honor, I enrolled my Dad (John Mitropoulos).  My father was the most wonderful, caring,
gentleman I have ever met and I have met quite a few including your Father and my Husband. - Written by daughter, Loretta Brown




Dad came over from Greece and landed in Kansas City, Missouri.  He met Jesse James' son there and like the name James and wanted something more American(?) and changed it from Mitropoulos to James.  Did he have a restaurant there?

He came to Philadelphia and met Anna K. Fiko and married her,  I was born May 30, 1937 and raised at 236 Maypole Rd in Upper Darby and attended St. Alice grade school.  I then went to the archdiocesan high school, which was West Catholic High School at 49 & Chestnut in Philadelphia.  I was on the dean's list but I am especially proud on not being absent or late for 4 years.  I took the local PTC or now SEPTA bus every morning and afternoon. After high school I went to Villanova College (now university) and received a Bachelor of Arts degree.  I met Father Falvey at Villanova and he gave me a scholarship to Villanova's School of Library Science.  This degree was not accredited by the American Library Association; so, I went to Drexel Institute of Technology(now Drexel University) and received my Masters in Informnation Science in 1970.  I worked at Villanova's Library full time for one year and several years on a part-time basis to supplement my income.  I worked at ISI (Institute for Scientific Information at 4th and Chestnut Streets for several years and managed the Science Citation Index and had 40 people working for me.  I needed more money and then landed a job at a lucrative company called Wyeth Laboratories and the rest is history. - Writen by son, John James




Hi Wil, Nice to hear from you and to know how interested you are in the Mitropoulos-James family.  You are correct Gianoukos was Sophia's maiden name.  My father did not seem to feel comfortable speaking about the past.  Perhaps it was WWII and the suffering the Greek people went through during those difficult war years.  As I understand it...Uncle Nicholas was the oldest child -- I think my father, John, was next -- and there were two sisters.  My Dad called one Carola.  There was another brother Athenasas (sp.) (Arthur) who was born after my Dad left Greece.  My father left his portion of whatever was coming to him from his parents to the brother he never knew.  My Father never dwelled in the past and perhaps it was because he missed them all.  Dad was all about his family here in the U.S. and his restaurant business.  He educated himself as best as he could by going to Night School (learning English and Accounting).  While he was living in Canton, Ohio, he was Treasurer of the Greek Orthodox Church.  While living in Kansas City, MO, his lawyer was Jesse James, Jr. - who was a lawyer and then a judge.  My father admired him with the exception of his drinking too much.  I think he saw what it was doing to him and for the rest of his life my father did not have more than an occasional drink. - Written by daughter, Loretta Brown

Left Patras, Greece, and took a boat called "Polonia" to New York, New York on April 17, 1913 and became a United States citizen on August 22, 1916 at Hartford, CT, USA.

notes: Merchant Marine WWII — highest per-capita casualty rate of any US service (1 in 26 killed, 733 ships sunk). Denied veteran status until Jan 19, 1988 (Schumacher v. Aldridge). Died Nov 19, 1989 — only 22 months after recognition. Service records at NARA Record Group 26, St. Louis (opened Dec 2019).

CORRECTION: Previous parents "Friedrick Von Schreyack" and "Hildergarde Von Schreyack" were FABRICATED by Frank Kimball Leland (~1930s). Correct father: Jacob Schreyack (b.1714). Correct mother: Barbara Wolf. NOTE: The existing GEDCOM incorrectly lists Jacob Schreyack as Catherine's father — he was actually HENRY's father (Schreyack→Shryock name evolution). Catherine's maiden name was Soloday.

persons.bioNotes:
Henry Shryock was an officer in the Revolutionary War. He was Lieutenant Colonel of the Sixth Battalion, under Colonel Otho Holland Williams. See McSherry's History of Maryland; page 382, Appendix B, and page 387, Appendix C.
See--"Historical Register of      the Officers of the Continental Army during the Revolutionary Apr. 1775 to Dec 1783." by T.B. Heitman of the
War Department, as follows: ---
"Shryock, Henry, (Md.) Lieut.  Colonel 1st Maryland Battalion of the Flying Camp June to Dec 1776. Lieut.  Colonel 6th. Maryland, Dec. 10 1776 to Apr. 17th. 1777."
There is in the possession of my father, General Thomas Jacob Shryock, the original letter of Henry Shryock to the Colonial Council, asknowledging his commission as Lieutenant Colonel of the 1st. Flying Camp. Also, a Treasury warrant for supplies taken by him for his troops.
Maryland Convention, April 28th. 1788. Signer of the Ratification of the Constitution of the United States of America, and as a delegate to the Maryland Convention. See McSherry's History of Maryland, page 402. Also, Scharf's History; Vol. 2, page 194. S.R.H.Reg.Gen.

When James J. Cosgrove was born in 1841, his father, Patrick, was 22 and his mother, Ellen, was 21. He married Winifred Gibbons in September 1870 in Shuylkill, PA. They had nine children in 16 years. He died on December 21, 1891, in Shenandoah, PA, at the age of 50, and was buried there.

Civil War vet. of 436 W. Center, Shenandoah, PA.

Winifred Gibbons was born on May 3, 1849, in Ireland, the daughter of Katherine and Peter. She married James J. Cosgrove in September 1870 in Schuylkill PA. They had nine children in 16 years. She died on March 18, 1933, in Shenandoah, PA, at the age of 83, and was buried there.
When Ella Cosgrove was born in 1870 in Shenandoah, PA, her father, James, was 29 and her mother Winifred, was 21. She ha 8 brothers. She died as a teenager on September 6, 1884, in her hometown.

notes: Birth year confirmed as 1873 (not 1883). Spanish-American War service — stationed San Francisco/Angel Island 1901 at age 27.
persons.bioNotes: When John Francis Cosgrove was born on June 19, 1883. in Shenandoah, PA, his father, James, was 32, and his mother, Winifred, was 24. He had 7 brothers and one sister. He married Sophia Barth on June 7th, 1906, in Delaware, and had one kid during their marriage. He went to fight in the Spanish American War and served in the military on April 1, 1901, in San Francisco, CA, when he was 27 years old. He died on June 14, 1933, in Philadelphia, PA, at the age of 59.

notes: Arrived Philadelphia April 15, 1847 (during Great Famine). Ship manifest likely on NARA Microfilm M425, Reel 64. Settled in Minersville, PA. Died Nov 24, 1873. Buried Saint Vincent de Paul Cemetery #1. NOT killed by Molly Maguires — likely died from cumulative mining work effects at age 54.
persons.bioNotes: When Patrick Cosgrove was born in 1819 in Cavan, Ireland, his father, John, was 39. He married Ellen Fitzgerald in 1840 in Ireland. They had five children in 19 years. He moved to Philadelphia, PA, on April 15, 1847. He died on November 24, 1873, at the age of 54, and was buried at Saint Vincent de Paul Cemetery #1 in Minersville, PA.

Picture of Mary and her granddaughter Mary Sheridan.

When Mary Cosgrove was born on March 18, 1845, in Swinford, Mayo, Ireland, her father, Patrick, was 26, and her mother, Ellen, was 25. She married James John Stanton in 1857 in Aghada, Cork, Ireland. They had 14 children in 23 years. She died on July 8, 1921, in Philadelphia, PA, at the age of 76, and was buried in Cheltenham, PA.

When Ellen Cosgrove was born in December 1856 in Somerset, PA, her father, Patrick, was 37, and her mother, Ellen, was 36. She had one son and five daughters with Hohn F. Reynolds between 1879 and 1888. She died in 1918 at the age of 62.

James John Stanton was born in 1845. He married Mary Cosgrove in 1857 in Aghada, Cork, Ireland. They had 14 children in 23 years. He moved around a lot. He was born in Mayo, Ireland, and got married there too. He later lived in Manchester, Lancashire, England, in 1961. Then, he moved back to Ireland to have three of his children in Ireland. In 1871, he was recorded living in Govan, Lanarksshire, Scotland. Came back to Shanandoah in 1874 and owned a piece of land  in Shenandoah; Mahanoy, Schuylkill, PA. He died on December 27, 1891, in Shenandoah, PA, when he was 46 years old.
Picture of her and her father, James John Stanton
